text.content:
After the morning evidence review, Caelith leads the party back up to the Library / Records Room (M5). Behind the writing desk at the window, a paneled section of the rear wall opens on a quiet hinge — Caelith demonstrates without comment — into a deeper records alcove the party did not see in Phase 1.

The alcove is small, warm, and lit by a single oil lamp. Two walls of low shelving hold bound civic records the public library does not carry: Yeomanry Bonded Firms — Charter Roll, Loftwick Treasury — Bonds Clerk Register, last fifteen years, Aerdi Commercial Marks & Counter-Marks (Rel Astra ed.), Grain Factor Annual Returns, and similar.

Trina sits at the small reading table, picks up the first relevant volume the party hands her, and quietly assists. She has clean handwriting and a steady index finger; she will help cross-reference if asked.

What the party can learn here

This scene resolves three identifications and one piece of background — variant Vandrell aliases. The DCs assume one PC is leading the search; Help actions and Trina's quiet assistance grant Advantage where appropriate.

Variant Vandrell aliases (background context)

  • Investigation DC 13 in the bound diplomatic-correspondence index: "Vandrell" appears as the surname of a junior court secretary in Aerdy three decades ago — almost certainly an unrelated family. But the entry yields three other "V---ll" variants in the same index, written in the same hand: Vendral, Vondrel, Voren. (The third, Voren, is the surname on the false letter of passage — Phase 2 evidence #2.)
  • Investigation DC 16: All three variants appear in unrelated incidents in the last twelve years — a customs dispute settled out of court, a missing-witness file dropped from prosecution, a guild-arbitration finding overturned on appeal. None of the three variants has a face on file. Each was, at some point, almost certainly Haskur Vandrell working under a different chop.
  • GM: The three Vandrell aliases are flavor — they confirm Caelith's framing that Haskur is a serial cover-identity who has been laundered through Aerdy commercial networks for a decade. They do not unlock new mechanics in Phase 2; they may resurface in Phase 3 as recognition keys.

Identifying Cindren & Vhal (the firm)

  • Investigation DC 12 in the Aerdi Commercial Marks volume: The crane-in-flight-above-three-coins seal (Phase 1 evidence #2 and #3) belongs to Cindren & Vhal, Bonded Factors, of Rel Astra, Hardby, and Loftwick.
  • Investigation DC 15: The tower-above-three-waves countermark (Phase 2 evidence #3) is also Cindren & Vhal — their internal clearing-mark, used between their own offices for tokens the firm has already weighed in.
  • History DC 14 in the Charter Roll: The firm's Loftwick presence is a satellite agent — a single notary office on Wick Lane, not a bonded branch. Their two main offices are Hardby and Rel Astra.
  • GM: See the Cindren & Vhal — Firm Dossier page on this journal.

Identifying Merro Gilst (the dead clerk)

  • Investigation DC 11 in the Treasury Bonds Clerk Register: "Gilst" resolves to Merro Gilst, junior bonds clerk, age 51, fifteen-year posting at the Loftwick Treasury, last entry stamped three days before the party returned to Loftwick. The stamp is followed by a small annotation, "posting closed."
  • Investigation DC 14 cross-reference: "Posting closed" in this register is the standard administrative phrase used when a clerk is removed from rolls due to death, retirement, or transfer to another paymaster. There is no retirement notation, and no transfer authorization. The closure is therefore a death — but the certifying physician's name (Reln Pollow) is not a Yeomanry coroner; he is a contract physician on Wick Lane.
  • Persuasion DC 13 with Caelith: Caelith confirms quietly that he had not yet been informed of Gilst's death through Audit Hall channels. He asks the party not to pursue Pollow until he has had the chance to read his licensure record.
  • GM: See the Merro Gilst — Dead Bonds Clerk page on this journal. The party may also choose to visit Gilst's rented room (see Gilst's Rented Room — Optional Investigation).

Identifying Tamsin Moraven (the missing grain factor)

  • Investigation DC 12 in the Grain Factor Annual Returns: "Moraven" resolves to Tamsin Moraven, senior grain factor, twelve years in trade, principal counting house on Tannerway. Last filed return four months ago, on time. No subsequent activity.
  • Investigation DC 15 cross-reference (returns + watch register): A short watch report is filed against Moraven's name dated eleven days ago — a missing-person inquiry submitted by her staff. The report is marked "open, unescalated." The Watch sergeant who took the report wrote, in careful Yeomanry shorthand: "Subject left counting house with bag and cloak; staff state she was due at a customer farm; staff cannot name the farm. No body. No struggle. Open file."
  • History DC 13: Moraven's filings include several queries against ledger entries on South Province grain shipments — exactly the "dry barony" export pattern implied by the hidden seed pouch (Phase 2 evidence #7). She had been the Yeomanry's most active questioner of false-crop receipts in the eastern grain trade.
  • GM: See the Tamsin Moraven — Missing Grain Factor page on this journal.

If Trina is asked about any of these names

  • Cindren & Vhal: she sets her finger flat on the page, lifts it, and says quietly: "Twice they came to me, and twice I said no, and the second time the man who asked stood a little too long in my doorway after he had been thanked. That is not how a clean firm leaves a house."
  • Merro Gilst: she shakes her head once. "I did not know him. I am sorry."
  • Tamsin Moraven: she pales, but speaks calmly. "Tamsin paid for half her daughter's tuition out of her own purse. I paid the other half. Tamsin does not vanish on her own initiative. Find her, please."

Time and pressure

The records alcove scene takes most of a morning in fiction. If the party works efficiently and rolls reasonably, all three identifications resolve before the second bell after noon. Inefficient work or repeated failed rolls advance the Pressure Clock by one segment (see Phase 2 — Departure → Pressure Clock).

What the party should leave the alcove with

  • The name Cindren & Vhal, Bonded Factors, with offices in Rel Astra (main), Hardby, and a Loftwick satellite at Wick Lane.
  • The fact that Merro Gilst is dead under suspicious paperwork, certified by a non-coronial physician.
  • The fact that Tamsin Moraven has been missing for eleven days, with an open but unescalated watch file.
  • The fact that Moraven was actively investigating false-crop receipts on the same grain trade Haskur's seed pouch points at.

None of this proves Cindren & Vhal of conspiracy. It does establish that the firm sits at the center of three converging threads, and that the threads are actively closing — Gilst dead, Moraven missing, the Vandrell escrow already drawn down.

pages.name: Cindren & Vhal, Bonded Factors — Firm Dossier

text.content:
What they are

A private bonded-factor firm: a banking house that specializes in escrow on chartered shipping, bonded commodity transfers (where the firm holds title until the cargo clears the destination port), and discreet off-balance-sheet instruments for clients who do not want their names on Aerdi state ledgers. Founded ~50 years ago by two Aerdi merchant cousins, both long dead.

Where they are

  • Rel Astra (main). A four-story counting house in the Old Harbor Ward, near the harbor commission and a short walk from the Goldsmiths' Quarter. Their full vault and senior partners' offices are here. The Old Harbor Ward is the oldest mercantile district in Rel Astra, and the firms there enjoy older privileges than the city's nominal authorities can easily revoke.
  • Hardby (branch). A two-story office in the merchants' quarter, handling Yeomanry-Aerdi trade clearing.
  • Loftwick (satellite). A single notary office on Wick Lane, staffed by one bonded clerk and one assistant. Not a full branch; cannot hold escrow, only originate paperwork. The Loftwick agent is named in the Charter Roll as Hesren Vesh.

Current leadership (as of Phase 2)

  • Veshen Cindren (Rel Astra, age 58, nephew of the founder). Senior partner. Public-facing. Hosts the firm's annual reception during the Festival of Lamps.
  • Tarlith Vhal Sereth (Rel Astra, age 64, no relation to the founders despite the name). Senior partner. Manages the off-balance-sheet instruments. Reclusive. Has not been seen at Festival of Lamps in eleven years.
  • Hesren Vesh (Loftwick agent). Junior bonded clerk; the party may visit his Wick Lane office if they wish (he will be polite, formal, and unhelpful — see GM note below).

What the firm officially does

  • Bonded storage for goods in transit between Aerdi ports and inland buyers.
  • Credit lines secured against held cargo, with clearing through their own house chop.
  • Arbitration of mercantile disputes between members of allied counting houses, by older custom rather than state law.

What the firm unofficially does

  • Laundering of receipts and instruments — moving paper between offices until provenance is no longer traceable to the issuer.
  • Identity shielding — providing letters of credit, letters of passage, and clearing chops for clients who do not wish their real names entered into Aerdi state registers.
  • Silence buying — small, regular cash payments to clerks, weighmasters, and watch sergeants who happen to sit near information the firm prefers undisturbed.

Caelith's working line on the firm: "Respectable enough for clean rooms. Useful enough for dirty ones."

If Haskur survived his fight, he arrived at one of these three doors as a client, not a stranger. Caelith says this plainly: "If Vandrell survived, he arrived as a client. The contingency that pulled him back was paid for. We are looking for the man who paid."

Reputation

Inside Aerdi commercial circles, Cindren & Vhal are known as discreet, expensive, and reliable. They charge a higher escrow margin than state-licensed banks, but they keep their clients' names off the Imperial Tax Register, which is itself a service.

Inside Yeomanry circles, the firm is regarded as tolerable but not preferred. The Hardby branch handles routine clearing for Yeomanry exporters at the kind of rates that suggest the firm wants Yeomanry business. The Loftwick satellite has, in the last two years, lost three quarters of bonded volume to Yeomanry-aligned banks. Trina describes their odor as "not yet rotten, but stored badly."

What the party can prove in Phase 2

  • The crane-in-flight-above-three-coins seal on the letter of credit (Phase 1 evidence #4) is a Cindren & Vhal seal.
  • The tower-above-three-waves countermark on the clipped silver coin (Phase 2 evidence #3) is a Cindren & Vhal internal clearing-mark.
  • The marginal endorsement "Hold for V., or his agent of record, against presentation" is in the same hand that signed Cindren & Vhal's Hardby clearing chops on the same instrument. Someone at Cindren & Vhal personally underwrote Haskur Vandrell six weeks before his fight.
  • The verb pattern in the ledger fragment ("settled / delayed / transferred") is Cindren & Vhal accounting shorthand.

What the party cannot yet prove

  • That the firm itself is conspiring. The marginal endorsement could be the work of one rogue partner, one suborned clerk, or one outside agent who got hold of firm wax.
  • Which partner authorized the Vandrell escrow. The Hardby chop is unsigned by initial.
  • What "Harbor Before Throne" means. The phrase implicates Cindren & Vhal in the timing of something, but Phase 2 does not say what throne or what harbor.

Visiting the Loftwick satellite (Wick Lane)

If the party walks to the Wick Lane office, Hesren Vesh receives them in a small front room. He is in his early thirties, wears the firm's livery (a plain dark-green coat with the crane-and-coins pin), and is courteous to the point of stiffness.

  • He confirms the firm's mark and seal in their published Charter Roll of Marks (a printed broadsheet anyone can buy at a bookseller).
  • He cannot open a discussion of any specific client account without a written authorization from Rel Astra. He says this politely, twice.
  • If the party shows him the marginal endorsement on the letter of credit, his eyes settle on it for two breaths longer than they should. Insight DC 14: the endorsement disturbs him and he is hiding it.
  • Persuasion DC 18 or Intimidation DC 17: he allows that the endorsement "reads like Hardby work, not Loftwick work," but he will not name a partner. He will not let the party take notes.
  • If pressed harder: he excuses himself, sends his clerk for refreshments, and uses the time to send a sealed message via courier to Rel Astra. The party may notice the courier leaving (Perception DC 14) but cannot intercept without breaking civility.

GM: Hesren is not a conspirator. He is a junior agent in a firm whose seniors are doing something he is not authorized to know about. Phase 2 does not resolve his role. He is a witness for Phase 3+.

What this implies for the next destination

The firm's senior partners are in Rel Astra. The marginal endorsement on the letter of credit is Hardby work. The destination east is therefore not yet a single location — it is a chain: Loftwick → Hardby → Rel Astra. Phase 2's pressure is to make the party take that chain quickly enough to outrun the conspiracy's response.

Who he was

Merro Gilst, age 51 at death, a junior bonds clerk at the Loftwick Treasury for fifteen years. Slight, balding, a careful eater of cheese. Unmarried. Lived in two rented rooms above a stationer's shop on Wick Lane (above the same building, in fact, that houses Caelith's own rooms — a small, dry coincidence that gives Caelith pause when he hears it).

His Treasury work was processing routine bond instruments — payments, releases, transfers. Nothing senior. His access, however, was broad: he saw every bond pass through the Treasury's clearing tables.

How he died

The official record: heart failure, certified at the scene three days before the party returned to Loftwick. The certifying physician was Reln Pollow, a contract physician with a small office two doors down from Gilst's lodgings.

The body was cremated within twelve hours — unusually fast even by Yeomanry custom, which permits same-day cremation only on the family's written request or under explicit coronial authorization. There was neither. The crematorium attendant signed the slip from a chit she does not, on closer inquiry, remember being given.

The Treasury Register entry reads "posting closed" with no transfer authorization, which under standard Yeomanry administrative practice means death.

Caelith, when the party puts the cremation timing together with the contract physician: "He was used. Then he was inconvenient. Then he was not anywhere we could examine."

What's wrong with the death

  • The Yeomanry maintains three coronial physicians on rota. None of the three was called. Pollow is a contract physician; he is licensed, but not on the coronial rota and not authorized to certify deaths-of-civic-record without coronial follow-up. (History DC 12 in the Yeomanry Bench Register confirms.)
  • Pollow's office is two doors down from Gilst's lodgings. He could be on scene within minutes of any call. This is convenient.
  • Pollow's licensure roll, if Caelith pulls it: he qualified in 575 CY in Hardby. He has practiced in Loftwick for nine years. He is not Yeomanry-born.
  • Gilst's name on the ledger fragment ("GILST transferred") suggests the firm regarded his payroll as already-migrated before his death. He was being paid by Cindren & Vhal, in shadow, possibly for years.

Why he was killed (GM-only)

Gilst had become a liability. As long as he was being paid by both sides, he was useful. The moment Cindren & Vhal needed to settle the Vandrell escrow without scrutiny, Gilst became the clerk who could flag the discrepancy in his sleep. "Transferred" in the firm's accounting verb meant he was being moved off active payroll; in fact he was being moved off the world.

The conspirators' instrument of removal: Pollow. The contract physician was paid to certify a heart failure on a man who, by the time he reached his rooms that evening, had drunk a tea spiked with a cardiac glycoside. The death looks natural; the certification is procedurally weak; the Yeomanry's coronial system is being maneuvered around, not corrupted.

Phase 2 should strongly imply all of this. It should not give the party a confession from Pollow. The party may want to confront Pollow; if they do, see GM note below.

Confronting Reln Pollow

Pollow is a soft, anxious man in his mid-forties with ink-stained fingers and a poorly-kept beard. If the party visits his office:

  • He confirms certifying Gilst's death. He says it was straightforward — Gilst was alone in his sitting room, half a cup of tea on the side table, dead in the chair.
  • Insight DC 14: he is more nervous than he should be about a routine certification three days old.
  • Insight DC 17: his nerves are not about the death; they are about something he expects to happen now. (He is right; Cindren & Vhal will dispose of him too if Phase 2 escalates.)
  • Persuasion DC 18 or Intimidation DC 16: he yields nothing actionable but starts to sweat. He says, "I did the work I was asked to do. I am sorry the man is dead. He was kind to me. He gave me a peach last summer."
  • If the party threatens to pull his license: he asks for two days to leave Loftwick. If granted, he packs that night and rides for Greyhawk in the morning. If denied: Pollow is poisoned the next evening by an unknown hand, and the party loses a witness. Caelith strongly recommends granting the two days.

What the party leaves with

  • Gilst was almost certainly murdered.
  • The murder was procedural, not violent — the Yeomanry's system was used against him, not its blades.
  • The killer is Pollow, but Pollow is the disposable instrument; the next ring out is the agent who paid Pollow, and that agent connects upward into Cindren & Vhal Hardby (or possibly Loftwick).
  • Phase 2 should not name the agent. The party may chase that thread to Hardby in Phase 3.

Gilst's rooms occupy the second floor above a stationer's shop on Wick Lane, three doors from where Caelith himself rents. The landlord is Doman Reith — a careful, anxious man in his sixties who has rented to clerks for thirty years and has not had a tenant die in his rooms before this one.

Getting in

The room has been sealed by the Yeomanry watch with a wax-and-string seal, but Caelith — once asked — provides a written request to Reith that the room be opened to the party for private inquiry, not for evidence transfer. Reith reads it twice, asks if there will be trouble, and unlocks the door.

Reith asks the party not to take anything. If they ask, he tells them: "A man came up here yesterday. He had a Watch tabard but I did not know him. He spent perhaps an hour. He left without telling me what he had found. I have not opened the door since."

  • Insight DC 13 on Reith: he is not lying. He is anxious because he does not know who the man-with-the-Watch-tabard was, and he suspects he should have refused.
  • Investigation DC 12 on Reith's description: the "Watch tabard" was Yeomanry pattern but the wrong colour ribbon at the cuff. Cross-referenced with the Watch's livery roster: the tabard was a real one purchased from a former courier, not stolen and not currently in service. Whoever wore it had bought it.
  • GM: The visitor was Merev Sarth (see Phase 2 Interruptions → Merev Sarth Retrieval Attempt) on a reconnaissance pass. She did not find Gilst's hidden material; she did not have time, and she did not know exactly what she was looking for. The party can succeed where she failed.

Layout

Two rooms: a sitting parlor that doubled as a study, and a small bedchamber. A water-closet under the stair. The parlor's east window looks down onto Wick Lane; the bedchamber's window looks onto a small courtyard with a wash-line.

The room has been searched. Drawers stand half-open; books are slightly out of order on their shelves; the carpet is rolled back at one corner and not laid flat again. Reith confirms it did not look like this when Gilst was alive.

What the searcher missed

Three things, each requiring a deliberate look:

A. The hollowed Constitutional Commentaries

  • Investigation DC 13 on the bookshelf: the third volume of Yeomanry Constitutional Commentaries vol. III sits half a finger forward of the others, as though replaced quickly. Gilst was a tidy man. The volume is hollowed; inside is a slim leather-bound receipts book.
  • The receipts book records, in Gilst's hand, sums received over the last four years from a payer recorded only as "H.V. Hardby" — sums totaling about 1,200 gp across forty-one entries. Always in cash, always at irregular intervals, always small enough to look like a side gig (private bookkeeping for an out-of-town client).
  • Investigation DC 14: Three of the forty-one entries are countersigned in a different hand with a single capital initial — "E." The countersigned entries are larger than the rest (one for 200 gp; the other two for 75 gp each) and irregular in timing relative to the H.V. cadence. Gilst's notes do not say what the countersignature means, only that the payments were larger and that he made no return correspondence on those days.
  • GM: "H.V. Hardby" is the Hardby branch of Hesren Vesh / Cindren & Vhal, but Gilst himself may not have known he was being paid by the firm proper; he likely thought he was on a junior partner's private retainer.
  • GM (campaign-level): The "E." countersignature is the party's first whisper of the actual paymaster above Cindren & Vhal — the same initial that has appeared in your Blooming Rot Part 1 notations. Phase 2 does not resolve who E. is. Caelith does not recognize the initial; Trina does not recognize the initial; no Yeomanry register lists a relevant figure who initials simply "E.". The party leaves with the letter and an open question. (Behind-the-scenes only — never read aloud: the initial belongs to Eclavdra of House Eilserv in Erelhei-Cinlu, the Drow city no living Yeoman has heard of. Do not name her in Phase 2 or Phase 3.)

B. The throat-lozenge tin

  • Perception DC 14 on the writing desk: a small decorative tin of throat lozenges. Real lozenges in the top half. The bottom of the tin is a false floor (Investigation DC 12 to feel the seam).
  • Under the false floor: a single signed bank instrument, undated, made out "to bearer," for 200 gp, drawn on Cindren & Vhal Hardby and stamped with the same Hardby chop as the Vandrell letter of credit. Same hand, same chop.
  • GM: This is the smoking-gun document. It proves Gilst was on Cindren & Vhal Hardby's private retainer, and it links his payroll directly to the Hardby chop on Vandrell's escrow. The party can carry this back to Caelith. He will, on seeing it, sit down for a moment and not speak.

C. The locket

  • Investigation DC 14 in the bedchamber: a small silver locket on a fine chain, hanging behind a framed sketch on the wall. Inside: a tiny painted portrait of an unidentified woman, perhaps thirty, dark-haired, with a slight smile. No name. No date.
  • GM: The locket is a personal mystery not resolved in Phase 2. Gilst was unmarried; the woman is not in any Yeomanry register. She may be a sister, a former love, or a daughter from before his Loftwick years. Phase 3+ may use this as a soft hook. Phase 2 leaves the locket as a quiet grief: the dead man had a private life nobody else knew.

D. The burned note in the cold hearth

  • Investigation DC 13 sifting the ashes: a corner of paper survives the burn, partially legible. In an unfamiliar hand, addressed to Gilst, four words remain: "Do not copy eastern marks twice."
  • GM: An instruction Gilst was given by the firm's intermediary. He was being told that any copy of an eastern clearing-chop he made was to exist once, on the actual instrument, and never as a working duplicate in his own files. The note is evidence that he was being managed, not just paid.

E. The coin shaving

  • Perception DC 15 along the writing-desk's seam: a single thin curl of silver no larger than a fingernail. Investigation DC 12: it is a coin shaving — the kind of debris produced when someone clips a coin to private weight, exactly as Phase 2 evidence #3 was clipped.
  • GM: Gilst was clipping coins for the firm — a small additional service, a small additional payment, a small additional thread of evidence the firm could pull on if he ever balked. Lone shavings drift to seams. He had clipped many.

F. The initials list

  • Investigation DC 13 in the receipts book's back cover: a separate small list, not in the main receipts hand. Five sets of initials, each followed by a small round mark like a thumb-press, and one of them simply: "E."
  • GM (campaign-level): the "E." here is the same "E." that countersigned three of Gilst's H.V. Hardby payments. The other four sets of initials are intermediaries — names Gilst never met but whose paydays he knew. Caelith does not recognize any of the initials. The party leaves with a list of letters and no faces. "E." is Eclavdra; do not name her, do not name Drow.

G. The fear note

  • Perception DC 12 inside the locket-frame: a folded slip of paper tucked behind the painted miniature. Three lines, in Gilst's own hand, undated:

"If they ask again, I do not know how to keep saying no.
If they stop asking, I do not know what that will mean.
This is the third winter I have been afraid."

  • GM: Gilst was coerced. Read in sequence with the other clues, the room reads as coercion → resistance → death: he was paid, then pressured, then asked to do something he tried to refuse, then quietly disposed of. Caelith, holding the slip: "He was using us before he was using them. He just ran out of time to tell us so."

What the party owes Reith

Reith asks them, when they leave, to seal the door again with their own wax and to keep his name out of any conversation that reaches the watch. He does not want trouble, and he is correct to be afraid. Caelith later sends Reith a small annual stipend through an unnamed channel; Reith never quite understands where the money comes from.

If the party brings the bank instrument to Caelith

Read aloud:

Caelith holds the slip of paper at arm's length, then closer. He sets it on the desk. He looks at Trina. Trina sets down her tea.

"That is a Hardby chop," Caelith says, very evenly. "That is the Hardby chop. The man who put that chop on this paper is the same man who put it on Vandrell's escrow. We now have a name for the next ring out. We do not have a face yet, but we have the hand."

He does not pick it up again. After a moment he says: "You may have just shortened our work by a season."

Who she is

Tamsin Moraven, mid-fifties, a senior grain factor in the Yeomanry. Twelve years in independent trade; before that, she clerked for the Loftwick Treasury's Grain Office. She grades and contracts harvest sales for export — most of her business is moving Yeomanry winter wheat and durum to Aerdi-coastal buyers. Her counting house is on Tannerway, near the eastern caravan yard.

Husband: Vellin Moraven, a quiet schoolmaster at a small private school. Eldest daughter: Ailen Moraven, mid-twenties, now studying with a sculptor in Hardby (her tuition co-sponsored years ago by Trina Alvere and Tamsin herself).

What she was investigating

For the last two years, Moraven had been filing increasingly pointed queries against ledger entries in the eastern grain trade. She specifically flagged:

  • Crop receipts for South Province buyers in volumes that exceeded the corresponding Yeomanry harvest filings.
  • Bonded shipments declared at Hardby for export that did not show up in Hardby's own weighbridge logs.
  • Three specific receipts dated in the spring of 581 that bore the Cindren & Vhal Hardby chop.

She did not yet have a written allegation; she had a pattern. She had begun corresponding with a Hardby weighmaster (name unknown to the party in Phase 2; possibly Veska Maelan, the trade-clearing notary Caelith named in Phase 1's forward hook).

What happened to her

Eleven days before the party returned to Loftwick, Moraven left her counting house in mid-afternoon with a travel bag and a heavy cloak. She told her staff she was riding to a customer's farm to verify a load. She did not name the farm. She did not return.

Her staff filed a watch report after three days. The Yeomanry watch sergeant who took the report, Sergeant Halir Anwic, marked it "open, unescalated" — the standard category for missing persons with no body, no struggle, and no political weight. The file has not moved since.

What the ledger fragment tells us about her

The ledger fragment recovered from Haskur's gear (Phase 2 evidence #6) lists her as "MORAVEN delayed." In Cindren & Vhal's accounting verbs, "delayed" means a held instrument awaiting countersignature — an account that is active, not closed. Read against a person rather than a paper, "delayed" reads as alive but contained.

Caelith is careful with this reading: "I do not say it as a comfort. I say it because the verb is not ‘settled.’ She is somewhere they have not yet decided to close her account. They will, eventually, decide."

Phase 2 commits to this reading. Tamsin is alive and being held — by whom and where remains a Phase 3 thread. The party's choice of travel approach affects how soon they can get to her.

The claims-before-loss pattern (GM-only)

If a PC follows Moraven's queries far enough — Investigation DC 16 against her counting house's bound carbons, or DC 14 if Trina assists — they uncover the most damning pattern Moraven was building toward:

The claims were filed before the loss occurred.

Insurance and indemnity claims on three South Province grain shipments — for storms, spoilage, and pirate seizure — were submitted to Yeomanry trade-clearing notaries with paperwork dated before the corresponding losses. In one case, by eleven days. In another, by three weeks.

Read forward, this is fraud. Read backward, this is something worse: the conspirators knew the losses were coming because they were arranging them. Prediction → profit → erasure.

Caelith, when shown the pattern: "That is not theft. That is being paid to know what tomorrow looks like. Whoever ran this had a way to make tomorrow obey them."

What the party can pursue in Phase 2

  • Visit her counting house on Tannerway. Her chief clerk, Doril Veth, will show them her last open ledger book. Investigation DC 12: the last three queries she filed all reference Cindren & Vhal Hardby chops. Investigation DC 15: her own copies of two of the queries are missing from the bound book; only the carbon impressions remain on the next page. Someone took the originals.
  • Visit her husband at home. Vellin Moraven receives the party in his small parlor. He has aged a year in eleven days. He produces, without prompting, a slip of paper Tamsin left on his desk the morning she disappeared: a single line in her hand reading "If I am not back by Sevenday, take Ailen out of Hardby." Vellin has not yet sent for Ailen because he could not bring himself to write the letter. (Insight DC 12: he should send for her tonight. Suggesting it gently is the right move.)
  • Visit Sergeant Halir Anwic at the watch house. Anwic is honest, overworked, and embarrassed about how thin his report is. He will reopen the file at Caelith's request, but not before. If asked, he will tell the party: "I did not look hard. I should have. I am looking now."

How Trina helps

Trina knows Tamsin slightly and Tamsin's daughter Ailen well. If the party visits Vellin Moraven, Trina offers to come along — not because she expects to extract more than the party can, but because Vellin will recognize her and will accept tea from her hand when he would not from a stranger.

Trina also offers, if asked: a private letter to Ailen Moraven in Hardby telling her to come home immediately. The letter goes out by Trina's own courier (a poet she sponsors who is leaving for Hardby that night). The party may, in Phase 3, find Ailen waiting at Vellin's house when they next visit Loftwick.
